perturbation.original_text: 9. TERM 9.1 This agreement shall be deemed to have commenced upon the date of last 6 signing hereof and it shall continue to bind the parti es for 3 (three) years after its termination. 9.2 This agreement shall be deemed to have become terminated in the event of the following (whichever happens the sooner): 9.2.1 the discussions between the parties being unsuccessful and accordingly breaking down; or 9.2.2 an agreement between the parties being concluded regarding their future relationship; or 9.2.3 in any event not later than one year after signing hereof unless extended by mutual agreement. 9.3 This clause will survive the cancellation of this agreement for what ever reason.
perturbation.changed_text: 9. TERM 9.1 This agreement shall be deemed to have commenced upon the date of last 6 signing hereof and it shall continue to bind the parti es for 3 (three) years after its termination. 9.2 This agreement shall be deemed to have become terminated in the event of the following (whichever happens the sooner): 9.2.1 the discussions between the parties being unsuccessful and accordingly breaking down; or 9.2.2 an agreement between the parties being concluded regarding their future relationship; or 9.2.3 in any event not later than six months after signing hereof unless extended by mutual agreement. 9.3 This clause will survive the cancellation of this agreement for what ever reason.
perturbation.explanation: This change introduces an in-text contradiction by altering clause 9.2.3 of the contract. The original clause states the agreement terminates no later than one year after signing, unless extended. The modification changes this duration to six months, creating a conflict with the original one-year timeframe. This contradiction creates uncertainty about the agreement's actual duration, causing potential disputes.
perturbation.contradicted_text: 9.2.3 in any event not later than six months after signing hereof unless extended by mutual agreement.
perturbation.justification: The changed text modifies clause 9.2.3, altering the termination date from 'one year' to 'six months'. The contradicted text, which is located at the same spot as the Changed Text, reflects this same change. Thus, the change creates a contradiction because the original text specifies a one-year timeframe, while the modified text specifies a six-month timeframe, creating uncertainty and a direct conflict within the contract as outlined in the perturbation explanation. This qualifies as an inconsistency leading to in-text contradiction as per the definition provided.

perturbation.original_text: 3.3 The parties agree that they will no t, during the course of their association with one another or, thereafter, disclose the information to any third party for any reason or purpose whatsoever without the prior written consent of the disclosing party, save in accordance with the provisions of this agreement. 3.4 Notwithstanding anything to the contrary contained, in this agreement the parties agree that the information may be disclosed by the receiving party to its professional advisors, agents and consultants, for the purposes of evaluating the investigation and enter into an agreement as contemplated in clause 1 (introduction), providing that the receiving party takes whatever steps are necessary to procure that such professional advisors, agents and consultants agree to abide by the terms of th is agreement to prevent the unauthorised disclosure of the information to third parties.
perturbation.changed_text: 3.3 The parties agree that they will not, during the course of their association with one another, disclose the information to any third party for any reason or purpose whatsoever without the prior written consent of the disclosing party, save in accordance with the provisions of this agreement. 3.4 Notwithstanding anything to the contrary contained, in this agreement the parties agree that the information may be disclosed by the receiving party to its professional advisors, agents and consultants, for the purposes of evaluating the investigation and enter into an agreement as contemplated in clause 1 (introduction), providing that the receiving party takes whatever steps are necessary to procure that such professional advisors, agents and consultants agree to abide by the terms of th is agreement to prevent the unauthorised disclosure of the information to third parties.
perturbation.explanation: The change omits 'or, thereafter,' from clause 3.3, which originally prohibited disclosing information to third parties during and after the parties' association without prior written consent. The modified text restricts disclosure only during the association, creating a contradiction because clause 3.4 allows disclosure to advisors for evaluation purposes even after association, but 3.3 seems to prevent that, causing ambiguity.
perturbation.contradicted_text: 3.4 Notwithstanding anything to the contrary contained, in this agreement the parties agree that the information may be disclosed by the receiving party to its professional advisors, agents and consultants, for the purposes of evaluating the investigation and enter into an agreement as contemplated in clause 1 (introduction), providing that the receiving party takes whatever steps are necessary to procure that such professional advisors, agents and consultants agree to abide by the terms of th is agreement to prevent the unauthorised disclosure of the information to third parties.
perturbation.justification: The changed text omits 'or, thereafter,' from clause 3.3, restricting disclosure only during the association. Clause 3.4, which is the contradicted text, allows disclosure to advisors for evaluation purposes even after association. The removal of 'or, thereafter' from 3.3 directly contradicts 3.4, creating ambiguity about whether disclosure is permitted after the association ends. This fits the definition of 'Omissions – In Text Contradiction' as essential language was removed creating a contradiction with obligations stated in other sections.

perturbation.original_text: 7. RETURN OF INFORMATION 7.1 The disclo sing party may at any time, request the receiving party to return any material containing, pertaining to or relating to information disclosed pursuant to the terms of this agreement and may, in addition request the receiving party to furnish a written stat ement to the effect that, upon such return, the receiving party has not retained in possession, or under its control, either directly or indirectly, any such material. 7.2 As an alternative to the return of the material contemplated in 7.1 above, the receivin g party shall, at the instance of the disclosing party, destroy such material and furnish the disclosing party with a written statement to the effect that all such material has been destroyed.
perturbation.changed_text: 7. RETURN OF INFORMATION 7.1 The disclo sing party may at any time, request the receiving party to return any material containing, pertaining to or relating to information disclosed pursuant to the terms of this agreement. 7.2 As an alternative to the return of the material contemplated in 7.1 above, the receivin g party shall, at the instance of the disclosing party, destroy such material and furnish the disclosing party with a written statement to the effect that all such material has been destroyed.
perturbation.explanation: The modified text creates an in-text contradiction by removing the phrase 'and may, in addition request the receiving party to furnish a written statement to the effect that, upon such return, the receiving party has not retained in possession, or under its control, either directly or indirectly, any such material.' from clause 7.1. This removes the disclosing party's right to request a written statement ensuring the receiving party doesn't retain the information. This contrasts with clause 7.2, which still requires a written statement if the receiving party destroys the material. The contradiction causes uncertainty over whether the receiving party must provide a written statement in both scenarios, creating ambiguity.
perturbation.contradicted_text: 7.2 As an alternative to the return of the material contemplated in 7.1 above, the receivin g party shall, at the instance of the disclosing party, destroy such material and furnish the disclosing party with a written statement to the effect that all such material has been destroyed.
perturbation.justification: The original text in 7.1 allowed the disclosing party to request a written statement from the receiving party confirming that no information was retained after the material's return. The changed text removes this right. Clause 7.2 still mandates a written statement if the receiving party destroys the material. The contradiction lies in the inconsistent requirement for a written statement; it's now required for destruction but not necessarily for return, creating ambiguity and a direct conflict in obligations within the contract as to when a written statement is needed from the receiving party.

perturbation.original_text: 18. POSTAL ADDRESSES 18.1 Any written notice in connection with thi s agreement may be addressed: 18.1.1 In the case of EnviroServ address P.O. Box 1547, Bedfordview 2008 telefax no (011) 454 6015 attention The financial director 18.1.2 In the case of PROSPECTIVE BUYER address telefax no attention 18.2 The notice shall be deemed to have been duly given: 18.2.1 7 days after posting, if posted by registered post to the party's address in terms of this sub -clause; 18.2.2 on delivery, if delivered to the party's physical address in terms of either this sub -clause or the next sub -clause dealing with serv ice of legal documents; 18.2.3 on despatch, if sent to the party's then telefax number and confirmed by registered letter posted no later than the next business day, unless the addressor is aware, at the time the notice would otherwise be deemed to have been giv en, that the notice is unlikely to have been received by the addressee through no act or omission of the addressee.
perturbation.changed_text: 18. POSTAL ADDRESSES 18.1 Any written notice in connection with thi s agreement may be addressed: 18.1.1 In the case of EnviroServ address P.O. Box 1547, Bedfordview 2008 telefax no (011) 454 6015 attention The financial director 18.1.2 In the case of PROSPECTIVE BUYER address telefax no attention 18.2 The notice shall be deemed to have been duly given: 18.2.1 14 days after posting, if posted by registered post to the party's address in terms of this sub -clause; 18.2.2 on delivery, if delivered to the party's physical address in terms of either this sub -clause or the next sub -clause dealing with serv ice of legal documents; 18.2.3 on despatch, if sent to the party's then telefax number and confirmed by registered letter posted no later than the next business day, unless the addressor is aware, at the time the notice would otherwise be deemed to have been giv en, that the notice is unlikely to have been received by the addressee through no act or omission of the addressee.
perturbation.explanation: This creates an in-text contradiction by changing the number of days after posting that a notice is deemed duly given. The original text states '7 days', while the changed text specifies '14 days'. This difference directly affects the time frame for legal notices, creating uncertainty about which period applies. If a critical notice is sent, it is ambiguous whether the receiving party has 7 or 14 days to act, potentially leading to disputes about contractual obligations, which are time-sensitive.
perturbation.contradicted_text: 18.2.1 14 days after posting, if posted by registered post to the party's address in terms of this sub -clause;
perturbation.justification: The changed text modifies section 18.2.1, altering the timeframe for deemed notice from '7 days' to '14 days' after posting. The contradicted text, also section 18.2.1, specifies '14 days'. Although the contradicted text and changed text are in the same location, the change introduced a contradiction in the document because the original text stated '7 days' while the contradicted/changed text now states '14 days'. Since the changed text introduces '14 days' after the original text stated '7 days', there is a clear inconsistency regarding the notice period, which causes an in-text contradiction based on the definitions above.

perturbation.original_text: 2. THE INFORMATION "Information" shall for the purpose of this agreement include, without limitation, any technical, commercial, scientific information, know -how, trade secrets, processes, machinery, designs, drawings, technical spe cifications, data in whatever form and information referred to in clause 1 (introduction) hereinabove communicated to the receiving party or acquired by the receiving party from the disclosing party during the course of the parties' association with one an other.
perturbation.changed_text: 2. THE INFORMATION "Information" shall for the purpose of this agreement include, without limitation, any technical, commercial, scientific information, know -how, trade secrets, processes, machinery, designs, drawings, technical spe cifications, data in whatever form communicated to the receiving party or acquired by the receiving party from the disclosing party during the course of the parties' association with one an other.
perturbation.explanation: This change involves removing the phrase "data in whatever form and information referred to in clause 1 (introduction) hereinabove" from the definition of 'Information' in Clause 2. This narrows the scope of what is considered 'Information' under the agreement. However, other clauses, such as Clause 3 which refers to information disclosed to one another, may still assume a broader definition, particularly Clause 1 (introduction), creating a contradiction. This impacts the enforceability of the agreement and causes significant ambiguity.
perturbation.contradicted_text: 3. DISCLOSURE OF INFORMATION 3.1 The parties agree to disclose the information to one another. 3.2 The parties acknowledge that the information is a valuable, special and unique 3 asset proprietary to the disclosing party. 3.3 The parties agree that they will no t, during the course of their association with one another or, thereafter, disclose the information to any third party for any reason or purpose whatsoever without the prior written consent of the disclosing party, save in accordance with the provisions of this agreement. 3.4 Notwithstanding anything to the contrary contained, in this agreement the parties agree that the information may be disclosed by the receiving party to its professional advisors, agents and consultants, for the purposes of evaluating the investigation and enter into an agreement as contemplated in clause 1 (introduction), providing that the receiving party takes whatever steps are necessary to procure that such professional advisors, agents and consultants agree to abide by the terms of th is agreement to prevent the unauthorised disclosure of the information to third parties. 3.5 The receiving party agrees: 3.5.1 not to utilise, employ, exploit or in any other manner whatsoever use the information disclosed pursuant to the provisions of this agreem ent for any purpose without the prior express written consent of the disclosing party. 3.5.2 that the unauthorised disclosure of the information to a third party may cause irreparable loss, harm and damage to the disclosing party. Accordingly, the receiving par ty indemnifies and holds the disclosing party harmless against any loss, action, claim, harm or damage, of whatever nature, suffered by the disclosing party pursuant to a breach by the receiving party of the provisions of this agreement,
perturbation.justification: The changed text narrows the definition of 'Information' by removing "data in whatever form and information referred to in clause 1 (introduction) hereinabove". Clause 3.4 of the contradicted text states that "the information may be disclosed by the receiving party to its professional advisors, agents and consultants, for the purposes of evaluating the investigation and enter into an agreement as contemplated in clause 1 (introduction)". The original definition included information referred to clause 1, but the changed definition does not, creating a contradiction as to what constitutes 'information' in this context, leading to an inconsistency.
